And so we kind of wake
Up in a new island every day, which is pretty incredible.
And they had naturalists on the boat with us.

And so I think one of the most amazing things is having that guide really kind of put everything into context for you to like understand what it is that makes the ecosystem unique to like help you understand what you're seeing.
So I think to me, like getting that deeper understanding was one of the coolest parts beyond just like, oh, my God, you're swimming with baby sea lions, which is like, oh, my God, that's cool.

Yeah, I could imagine having that person kind of educating you in a social way is much better than having like your nose and like a guidebook the whole time trying to like figure stuff out because you want to be learning and understanding what you're seeing and stuff.
Yeah.
Yeah.

And they're talking about, you know, how like the ocean, like learning how ocean currents work with the cold and warm air, which is like, or cold and warm water, which is how you get this kind of unique ecosystem.

Graphic episode.

get kind of like nervous around the big fish.

So how many islands are there?
We, I think we, they sort of, they often split it between like kind of the east and the west Galapagos.
And often you'll take a trip kind of on one or the other.
I'm not sure if one's like better than the other.
I think we were on the kind of eastern side and we mostly sailed around like, I think there was like four or five main islands.
And then there's a lot like smaller ones kind of off of there.
But it's such a wild landscape because some of it is so new because it's.
here's
So the volcanic activity is so active.

And then you have other islands that are kind of much more established, know, old volcanoes.
But I think because it's active, you know, it's like there would be volcanoes, islands that were separate islands, but then they became one island.
So you have these unique populations of birds and stuff on either side of the island.
So you can understand why Darwin showed up and felt like he had finally like found the place to study his theories.
But yeah, that part is a, it is pretty crazy.
Like there was some event, I think, like in the last hundred years where because of that volcanic activity, I guess the seabed actually rose like 30 feet.

And so this whole coral reef just lifted up and it was so fast that I guess someone showed up to the island and there were like dead sharks and dead fish all over it because they hadn't been able to escape this event.
So it is just like, it's such a wild, wild landscape.
